title: Project selection max closure
description: Solve project selection using min cut

examples.explanation:
Projects: 0(profit=5), 1(cost=2, prerequisite=0), 2(profit=3, prerequisite=1). Generate all 8 subsets (0-7):
Mask 0 (000): subset={}, profit=0-(5+2+3)= -10? Wait, careful: profit = sum(selected profits) = 0, unselected: project0 profit5 (not selected so not added), project1 cost2 (not selected so not subtracted? Actually, profit calculation: total = sum(profits[i] for i in selected) + sum(profits[j] for j not selected where profits[j] < 0? No—standard formulation: profit = sum of profits of selected projects. Costs are negative profits. So unselected projects don't contribute. So profit=0. Check closure: empty set trivially satisfies. Profit=0.
Mask 1 (001): subset={0}, profit=5. Check closure: project0 has no prerequisites, so valid. Profit=5.
Mask 2 (010): subset={1}, profit= -2. Check closure: project1 requires project0, but 0 not selected → invalid.
Mask 3 (011): subset={0,1}, profit=5+(-2)=3. Check closure: project0 ok, project1 requires 0 (selected) → valid. Profit=3.
Mask 4 (100): subset={2}, profit=3. Check closure: project2 requires project1, not selected → invalid.
Mask 5 (101): subset={0,2}, profit=5+3=8. Check closure: project0 ok, project2 requires 1, not selected → invalid.
Mask 6 (110): subset={1,2}, profit=(-2)+3=1. Check closure: project1 requires 0 not selected → invalid.
Mask 7 (111): subset={0,1,2}, profit=5+(-2)+3=6. Check closure: project0 ok, project1 requires 0 (selected), project2 requires 1 (selected) → valid. Profit=6.
Maximum profit among valid subsets: max(0,5,3,6)=6.

Projects: 0(cost=1, prerequisite=1), 1(profit=2). Generate 4 subsets:
Mask 0 (00): subset={}, profit=0, closure valid. Profit=0.
Mask 1 (01): subset={0}, profit= -1. Check closure: project0 requires project1, not selected → invalid.
Mask 2 (10): subset={1}, profit=2. Check closure: project1 has no prerequisites, valid. Profit=2.
Mask 3 (11): subset={0,1}, profit= -1+2=1. Check closure: project0 requires 1 (selected), project1 ok → valid. Profit=1.
Maximum profit: max(0,2,1)=2.

approaches.name: Brute Force Enumeration
approaches.summary: Enumerate all possible subsets of projects and check which ones satisfy closure constraints (all prerequisites included). Calculate profit for each valid subset and track maximum. Only practical for very small n.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Enumeration
ladders.desc: Enumerate all subsets of projects, check closure constraints, and find maximum profit.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute-force solution to the project selection problem with closure constraints. The intuition is that we need to consider every possible combination of projects (2^n possibilities) because we're looking for the optimal subset that satisfies the closure property: if a project is selected, all its prerequisites must also be selected. We generate all subsets using bitmask representation (0 to 2^n-1), where each bit position corresponds to a project. For each subset, we verify closure by checking that for every selected project, all its prerequisite projects are also selected. If the subset satisfies closure, we calculate its total profit by summing profits of selected projects (positive values) and costs of unselected projects (negative values, treated as costs). We track the maximum profit across all valid subsets. The time complexity is O(2^n * n) because we iterate through 2^n subsets and for each subset, we check closure (O(n) worst-case by checking all projects' prerequisites) and calculate profit (O(n)). Space complexity is O(n) for storing the prerequisites and profits. This approach is only practical for n ≤ 20-25 due to exponential growth. Connection to real-world: This mirrors exhaustive evaluation of all possible project portfolios when dependencies must be respected. Common mistakes include: 1) Forgetting to handle negative profits (costs) correctly—unselected projects with negative values reduce profit, 2) Incorrect closure check—must ensure ALL prerequisites of ALL selected projects are included, not just direct ones, 3) Off-by-one errors in bitmask iteration. Real code pattern: Use integer bitmask from 0 to (1<<n)-1, check closure with bitwise AND operations, calculate profit by iterating through all projects.

Actually, profit calculation: total = sum(profits[i] for i in selected) + sum(profits[j] for j not selected where profits[j] < 0? No—standard formulation: profit = sum of profits of selected projects. Costs are negative profits. So unse
... (simplified)

Projects: 0(cost=1, prerequisite=1), 1(profit=2). Generate 4 subsets:
Mask 0 (00): subset={}, profit=0, closure valid. Profit=0.
Mask 1 (01): subset={0}, profit= -1. Check closure: project0 requires project1, not selected → invalid.
Mask 2 (10): subset={1}, profit=2. Check closure: project1 has no prerequisites, valid. Profit=2.
Mask 3 (11): subset={0,1}, profit= -1+2=1. Check closure: project0 requires 1 (selected), project1 ok → valid. Profit=1.
Maximum profit: max(0,2,1)=2.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Closure Check / Discharge
ladders.desc: Check if a given subset of projects satisfies the closure property.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine verifies whether a selected set of projects satisfies the closure constraint: for every project in the subset, all its prerequisite projects must also be in the subset. The intuition is that project dependencies must be respected—you cannot select a project without also selecting everything it depends on. This is a feasibility check that ensures the subset represents a valid project portfolio. The algorithm takes a bitmask representing the subset (n bits, where bit i=1 if project i is selected) and a list of prerequisite sets. It iterates through all projects i (0 to n-1). For each project i that is selected (bit i=1), it checks that all prerequisites in prerequisites[i] are also selected (bit j=1 for all j in prerequisites[i]). If any prerequisite is missing, the subset is invalid. Time complexity is O(n * p) where p is the maximum number of prerequisites per project, but in worst case p=O(n), so O(n^2). However, with efficient bit operations, we can do O(n) by checking each project's prerequisites against the bitmask. Space complexity is O(1) extra space. Connection to L0: This is the core validation step in the brute-force enumeration—without it, we might consider invalid subsets. Common mistakes: 1) Not checking all selected projects—only checking prerequisites for some projects, 2) Incorrectly handling empty prerequisite lists, 3) Using slow list lookups instead of bitwise AND for efficiency. Real code pattern: Use (mask >> i) & 1 to check if project i is selected, and (mask >> j) & 1 to check prerequisites.

examples.trace:
Projects: 0: prerequisites=[], 1: prerequisites=[0], 2: prerequisites=[1]. Mask 011 (binary) means projects 0 and 1 selected, project 2 not selected.
Check each selected project:
- Project 0 selected: prerequisites empty → ok.
- Project 1 selected: prerequisites=[0], check if project 0 selected → yes (bit 0=1).
All selected projects satisfy closure → return true.

Mask 101: projects 0 and 2 selected, project 1 not selected.
Check selected projects:
- Project 0: prerequisites empty → ok.
- Project 2: prerequisites=[1], check if project 1 selected → no (bit 1=0).
Closure violated → return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Subset Enumeration / Push-Relabel
ladders.desc: Generate all possible subsets of n projects using bitmask representation.
ladders.explanation: This core logic involves generating every possible subset of a set of n projects. The intuition is that we need to explore all combinations to find the optimal one, and bitmasks provide an efficient way to represent subsets as integers. Each project is assigned a bit position (0 to n-1), and a subset is represented by an integer mask where bit i is 1 if project i is included, 0 otherwise. There are exactly 2^n possible subsets, corresponding to integers from 0 to 2^n - 1. The algorithm simply iterates through all masks from 0 to (1<<n)-1. For each mask, we can extract which projects are selected using bit operations. This is fundamental to brute-force solutions for subset problems. Time complexity is O(2^n) to generate all masks, and O(n) to interpret each mask if needed, but the generation itself is O(2^n). Space complexity is O(1) for the iteration. Connection to L1: This enumeration provides the subsets that need to be checked for closure. Common mistakes: 1) Off-by-one in the loop range—must go up to (1<<n)-1 inclusive, 2) Misinterpreting bit positions, 3) Not handling n=0 correctly. Real code pattern: Use for mask in range(1<<n): then inside, use (mask >> i) & 1 to check membership.

ladders.title: Building Block — Flow Network Construction
ladders.desc: Compute total profit for a given subset of projects based on individual profits/costs.
ladders.explanation: This building block calculates the total profit of a selected subset of projects. Each project has an associated profit (positive value) or cost (negative value). The intuition is that when we select a set of projects, we gain their profits and avoid their costs if we don't select them? Wait—clarify: In standard project selection, profit is sum of profits of selected projects. Unselected projects do not contribute to profit. So if a project has negative profit (a cost), selecting it reduces total profit, not selecting it means we don't incur that cost. Thus, total profit = sum of profits[i] for all i in the subset. The algorithm takes a bitmask representing the subset and an array profits of length n. It initializes total=0, iterates i from 0 to n-1, and if project i is selected (checked via (mask >> i) & 1), it adds profits[i] to total. Time complexity is O(n) for iterating through all projects. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L2: After enumerating a subset, we need to compute its profit to compare with the maximum. Common mistakes: 1) Adding profits for unselected projects, 2) Misinterpreting negative profits—they are simply added (so -5 reduces total), 3) Not handling empty subset correctly (profit=0). Real code pattern: Use a loop and bit check, then total += profits[i] if selected.

examples.trace:
Mask 011 (binary) → projects 0 and 1 selected, project 2 not selected.
profits=[5, -2, 3]
Initialize total=0.
Check each project:
- i=0: selected? (011 >> 0) & 1 = 1 → yes, add profits[0]=5 → total=5.
- i=1: selected? (011 >> 1) & 1 = 1 → yes, add profits[1]=-2 → total=3.
- i=2: selected? (011 >> 2) & 1 = 0 → no, skip.
Total profit=3.

Mask 101 → projects 0 and 2 selected, project 1 not selected.
profits=[-1, 2, -3]
total=0.
- i=0: selected? (101 >> 0) & 1 =1 → add -1 → total=-1.
- i=1: selected? (101 >> 1) & 1 =0 → skip.
- i=2: selected? (101 >> 2) & 1 =1 → add -3 → total=-4.
Profit=-4.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bitmask / Residual Updates
ladders.desc: Use bitwise operations to check if an element is in a subset represented as an integer.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves using bitwise operators to manipulate and query subsets represented as bitmasks. A bitmask is an integer where each bit corresponds to an element in a set: bit i (0-indexed) is 1 if element i is included, 0 otherwise. The intuition is that integers in binary form naturally encode subset membership, and bitwise operations allow efficient checks and modifications. Key operations: 1) Check if element i is in the subset: (mask >> i) & 1 returns 1 if bit i is set, 0 otherwise. 2) Add element i to subset: mask | (1 << i). 3) Remove element i: mask & ~(1 << i). 4) Toggle element i: mask ^ (1 << i). For this problem, we primarily use the check operation. Time complexity for each operation is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This is how we determine which projects are selected when calculating profit. Common mistakes: 1) Using wrong bit shift direction, 2) Forgetting to use parentheses in bit operations, 3) Off-by-one in bit indexing (should start at 0). Real code pattern: Use (mask >> i) & 1 == 1 to check membership.

examples.trace:
Mask in binary: 1010 (bits: position 3=1, 2=0, 1=1, 0=0). Check element i=1:
- Compute mask >> i: 1010 >> 1 = 0101 (binary).
- Then (mask >> i) & 1: 0101 & 0001 = 0001 (binary) which is 1.
Since result is 1, element 1 is in the subset.

Mask: 0110. Check i=0:
- mask >> 0 = 0110 (no shift).
- (0110) & 0001 = 0000 = 0.
Result is 0, so element 0 is not in the subset.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Subsets & Flow Networks
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of subsets, dependencies, and max-flow/min-cut reduction for project selection.
ladders.explanation: Project selection with closure: select subset maximizing profit while if a project is selected, all prerequisites must be selected. Reduced to max-flow/min-cut: source to positive profit projects, negative to sink, infinite edges for dependencies. Min cut gives optimal selection.

examples.trace:
Set has 2 elements: X and Y.
For each element, choose include or exclude:
- For X: include or exclude (2 choices).
- For Y: include or exclude (2 choices).
Total combinations: 2 * 2 = 4.
List subsets: 1) {} (exclude both), 2) {X} (include X, exclude Y), 3) {Y} (exclude X, include Y), 4) {X,Y} (include both).

Elements: A, B, C.
Choices per element: 2 each.
Total: 2 * 2 * 2 = 8.
Subsets: {}, {A}, {B}, {C}, {A,B}, {A,C}, {B,C}, {A,B,C}.

approaches.name: Push-Relabel with Heuristics
approaches.summary: Use the push-relabel algorithm with gap and global relabeling heuristics on the same flow network reduction. This provides better practical performance for dense dependency graphs compared to Dinic's algorithm.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Push-Relabel with Heuristics
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of project selection using push-relabel max flow reduction with gap and global relabeling heuristics.
ladders.explanation: This solves the project selection problem: given projects with profits and dependencies, select projects to maximize total profit while respecting dependencies. We reduce it to a max flow/min cut problem. Create a flow network: source connects to profitable projects (capacity = profit), unprofitable projects connect to sink (capacity = -profit), and dependencies have infinite capacity. The min cut gives optimal selection. We implement push-relabel with gap and global relabeling heuristics for O(n²√m) performance. Intuition: push-relabel maintains height labels and excess flow, pushing flow downhill and relabeling when stuck. Gap heuristic detects disconnected nodes, global relabeling periodically recomputes heights from sink for efficiency. Steps: 1) Build network with n+2 nodes. 2) Initialize heights: source at n, others at 0. 3) Preflow: saturate source edges. 4) While active nodes exist: discharge active node (push/relabel). 5) Apply gap heuristic when height gap detected. 6) Periodically global relabel. 7) Compute min cut from reachable nodes. Time: O(n²√m) with heuristics, space: O(n²) for adjacency matrix. Connection: This integrates all lower-level components. Common mistakes: forgetting infinite capacity for dependencies, incorrect height initialization, not handling negative profits properly. Real code pattern: adjacency matrix for dense graphs, active node queue, height and excess arrays.

examples.trace:
1) Build network: nodes: s(0), t(5), projects 1-4. 2) Add edges: s→1 cap=5, s→3 cap=2, 2→t cap=3, 4→t cap=1, inf edges: 1→3, 2→4. 3) Initialize heights: h[s]=4, others=0. 4) Preflow: push 5 from s→1, push 2 from s→3. Excess: node1=5, node3=2. 5) Discharge node1: height=4, neighbor3 height=0, push min(5,inf)=5 to node3. Excess: node1=0, node3=7. 6) Discharge node3: height=0, neighbor t height=0 (no push), relabel to 1. Push to t: min(7,2)=2, excess: node3=5. 7) Continue: node3 pushes to node4 via inf edge, node4 pushes to t. 8) Global relabel af
... (simplified)

1) Network: s→1 cap=10, s→4 cap=8, 2→t cap=5, 3→t cap=2, inf: 1→2, 3→4. 2) Heights: h[s]=4. 3) Preflow: push 10 to node1, 8 to node4. Excess: node1=10, node4=8. 4) Discharge node1: push 10 to node2 via inf. Node2 excess=10. 5) Node2: push min(10,5)=5 to t, excess=5. Relabel node2 to 1. 6) Node4: push 8 to node3 via inf, node3 excess=8. 7) Node3: push min(8,2)=2 to t, excess=6. Relabel to 1. 8) Global relabel updates heights. 9) Continue pushes: node2 pushes to node3? No edge. Node3 pushes to node4? No. 10) Gap heuristic: heights stabilize. 11) 
... (simplified)

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Closure Check / Discharge
ladders.desc: Implement the discharge operation for push-relabel with gap and global relabeling heuristics.
ladders.explanation: The discharge operation processes an active node (with excess flow) until it becomes inactive. For a node u, while excess[u] > 0: iterate through neighbors. If height[u] = height[v] + 1 and residual capacity > 0, push flow: amount = min(excess[u], cap[u][v]). Update excess and residual capacities. If no such neighbor, relabel: increase height[u] to 1 + min height of neighbors with residual capacity. Gap heuristic: if after relabeling, height[u] creates a gap (no nodes at some height k), set heights > k to n+1 (effectively removing them). Global relabeling: periodically (e.g., every n steps) recompute heights from sink using BFS for accuracy. Intuition: discharge locally pushes flow downhill, relabels when stuck; gap removes disconnected nodes; global relabeling prevents height inflation. Time: O(n) per discharge, O(n²) total with heuristics. Space: O(n²). Connection: This is the core loop in L0. Common mistakes: not checking residual capacity, incorrect gap detection, infrequent global relabeling. Real code pattern: while excess[u] > 0: for v in neighbors: if h[u]==h[v]+1 and cap[u][v]>0: push; if no push: relabel; check gap; if global relabel trigger: BFS from t.

examples.trace: 1) u=1, excess=5, height=2. 2) Check neighbor 0: height 4, not height[1]+1. 3) Neighbor 2: height 1, yes height[1]=height[2]+1? 2=1+1, yes. cap[1][2]=3>0. Push min(5,3)=3. Update: excess[1]=2, excess[2]=3, cap[1][2]=0. 4) Excess[1]=2>0. Check neighbors: 0 no, 2 cap=0. Relabel: min height of neighbors with cap>0? None, so height[1]=1+min()? Set to n+1=4. Gap check: heights now [4,4,1,0], gap at height 2,3? Yes, set heights>1 to 4. 5) Global relabel not triggered. Final: excess[1]=2, height[1]=4.

1) u=2, excess=4, height=2. 2) Neighbor 1: height 2, not height[2]+1. 3) Neighbor 3: height 0, yes 2=0+1? No. Relabel: min height of neighbors with cap>0: neighbor3 height=0, cap[2][3]=3>0. So height[2]=0+1=1. 4) Now height[2]=1, neighbor3 height=0, yes 1=0+1. Push min(4,3)=3. Excess[2]=1, excess[3]=3, cap[2][3]=0. 5) Excess[2]=1>0. Check neighbors: no push possible. Relabel: no neighbors with cap>0, height[2]=1+min()=4. Gap check: heights [3,2,4,0], gap at height 1,3? Set heights>0 to 4? Not applied. 6) Global relabel if triggered. Final: excess[2]=1, height[2]=4.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Subset Enumeration / Push-Relabel
ladders.desc: Implement the basic push and relabel operations for the push-relabel algorithm.
ladders.explanation: Push operation: moves flow from a node u to a neighbor v if: height[u] = height[v] + 1 and residual capacity cap[u][v] > 0. Amount = min(excess[u], cap[u][v]). Update excess[u] -= amount, excess[v] += amount, cap[u][v] -= amount, cap[v][u] += amount. Relabel operation: increases height of node u when it has excess but no admissible neighbor (no v with height[u] = height[v] + 1 and cap[u][v] > 0). New height[u] = 1 + min{height[v] : cap[u][v] > 0}. If no such v, set height[u] = n (number of nodes). Intuition: push sends flow downhill like water, relabel raises height to create a downhill path. This ensures flow moves toward sink. Time: O(1) per push/relabel. Space: O(1). Connection: These are used in L1's discharge. Common mistakes: not updating residual capacities bidirectionally, incorrect height condition, not handling isolated nodes in relabel. Real code pattern: def push(u,v): if h[u]==h[v]+1 and cap[u][v]>0: flow = min(excess[u], cap[u][v]); excess[u]-=flow; excess[v]+=flow; cap[u][v]-=flow; cap[v][u]+=flow. def relabel(u): min_h = inf; for v in neighbors: if cap[u][v]>0: min_h = min(min_h, h[v]); h[u] = min_h + 1 if min_h!=inf else n.

examples.input: Push: u=0, v=1, excess[0]=5, cap[0][1]=3, heights: [2,1,0]
examples.trace: 1) Check: height[0]=2, height[1]=1, 2=1+1 yes. cap[0][1]=3>0. 2) Amount = min(5,3)=3. 3) Update: excess[0]=5-3=2, excess[1]=0+3=3. 4) Residual: cap[0][1]=3-3=0, cap[1][0]=0+3=3.
1) u=2 has excess? Assume yes. 2) Find neighbors with cap>0: neighbor 1 has cap[2][1]=4>0, height[1]=2. 3) min height = 2. 4) New height[2] = 2 + 1 = 3.

testCases.input: Push: u=1, v=2, excess[1]=4, cap[1][2]=2, heights: [3,2,1]
Push: u=0, v=1, excess[0]=3, cap[0][1]=5, heights: [1,0,0]
Push: u=2, v=0, excess[2]=6, cap[2][0]=0, heights: [2,1,3]
Push: u=0, v=2, excess[0]=7, cap[0][2]=4, heights: [2,1,1]
Push: u=1, v=0, excess[1]=5, cap[1][0]=5, heights: [0,1,0]
Push: u=2, v=1, excess[2]=3, cap[2][1]=3, heights: [2,1,2]

ladders.title: Building Block — Flow Network Construction
ladders.desc: Construct the flow network graph from project profits and dependencies.
ladders.explanation: Given projects with profits (positive or negative) and dependencies (u requires v), build a flow network for max flow reduction. Create nodes: source s, sink t, and one node per project. For each project i with profit p_i: if p_i > 0, add edge s→i with capacity p_i; if p_i < 0, add edge i→t with capacity -p_i. For each dependency (u,v): add edge u→v with infinite capacity (or a large number like sum of positive profits + 1). Intuition: selecting project i with profit p_i > 0 contributes p_i to profit, but if not selected, we 'pay' p_i in cut. Similarly, negative profits cost if selected. Dependencies enforce that if u is selected, v must be selected (infinite edge ensures cut includes both or neither). The min cut gives optimal selection. Steps: 1) Let n = number of projects. 2) Initialize adjacency matrix of size (n+2)x(n+2). 3) For i in 0..n-1: if profit[i] > 0: cap[s][i] = profit[i]; else: cap[i][t] = -profit[i]. 4) For each (u,v) in dependencies: cap[u][v] = INF. Time: O(n² + m) for matrix, space: O(n²). Connection: This network is input to L2. Common mistakes: using wrong indices, not handling infinite capacity properly, missing source/sink edges. Real code pattern: n_proj = len(profits); cap = [[0]*(n_proj+2) for _ in range(n_proj+2)]; s = n_proj; t = n_proj+1; for i,p in enumerate(profits): if p>0: cap[s][i]=p; else: cap[i][t]=-p; for u,v in deps: cap[u][v]=INF.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Bitmask / Residual Updates
ladders.desc: Update residual capacities in an adjacency matrix during flow pushes.
ladders.explanation: In max flow algorithms, when flow is pushed along an edge, we update the residual capacities to reflect remaining capacity and allow flow reversal. For an edge u→v with capacity c, if we push flow f (0 ≤ f ≤ c), then: residual capacity from u to v becomes c - f, and residual capacity from v to u becomes f (initially 0). This is stored in an adjacency matrix cap where cap[u][v] is residual capacity from u to v. Operation: cap[u][v] -= f; cap[v][u] += f. Intuition: This maintains the invariant that flow can be sent back along reverse edges to undo previous pushes, enabling algorithms to find optimal flow. It's essential for push-relabel and other max flow methods. Steps: 1) Check f ≤ cap[u][v]. 2) Update cap[u][v] -= f. 3) Update cap[v][u] += f. Time: O(1). Space: O(1). Connection: Used in L3's network and L2's push. Common mistakes: not updating reverse edge, pushing more than capacity, using integer overflow for infinite capacities. Real code pattern: def update_residual(u,v,f): cap[u][v] -= f; cap[v][u] += f.

examples.input: cap matrix: [[0,5],[0,0]], push f=3 from u=0 to v=1
cap matrix: [[0,0,0],[4,0,0],[0,2,0]], push f=2 from u=1 to v=0

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Subsets & Flow Networks
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of subsets, dependencies, and max-flow/min-cut reduction for project selection.
ladders.explanation: Project selection with closure: select subset maximizing profit while if a project is selected, all prerequisites must be selected. Reduced to max-flow/min-cut: source to positive profit projects, negative to sink, infinite edges for dependencies. Min cut gives optimal selection.

examples.trace: 1) n=3, create 3x3 matrix of zeros. 2) Edge (0,1,5): set matrix[0][1] = 5. 3) Edge (1,2,3): set matrix[1][2] = 3. 4) Other cells remain 0.
